task_summary: Longitudinal qualitative analysis: extract themes per wave, track theme evolution, and produce comparative summaries
csv_content:
respondent_id,wave,community,age_band,response_text
R-W1-001,1,Community-A,45-64,"The bus stops running at 6 and the GP closes at 5:30. Even when I want to go I just can't get there. I'd take a Saturday slot but they're booked weeks out."
R-W1-002,1,Community-A,65+,"I can't really afford the prescriptions on my pension so I take half doses. Doctor doesn't know."
R-W1-003,1,Community-B,18-29,"My English is okay for everyday things but the medical words I get lost in. I don't want to look stupid asking."
R-W1-004,1,Community-B,30-44,"I work nights so taking my kid in the day means I either skip sleep or skip work and I can't skip work."
R-W1-005,1,Community-C,45-64,"They never look at me. They look at their screens. After a while you stop trying."
R-W1-006,1,Community-C,30-44,"It's not the cost, it's that nobody at the practice speaks like my grandmother did. It feels like a different country."
R-W2-001,2,Community-A,45-64,"Still the same — the buses are worse if anything. I gave up trying to make appointments after a while."
R-W2-002,2,Community-A,65+,"My daughter's started picking up the prescriptions for me which helps, but I still skip doses some weeks."
R-W2-003,2,Community-B,18-29,"I downloaded the translation app on my phone which helps a bit, but I still feel like I'm holding up the appointment."
R-W2-004,2,Community-B,30-44,"They opened a Saturday clinic but it's first-come-first-served and I have to queue from 7am with the kids."
R-W2-005,2,Community-C,45-64,"There's a community health worker now who comes in Tuesdays. She's the only one I'll see. Otherwise I don't go."
R-W2-006,2,Community-C,30-44,"The new receptionist is nicer. Small thing but it matters. I went back this year."
R-W3-001,3,Community-A,45-64,"They've started a community minibus service to the surgery on Thursdays. I've been twice. It's working — just don't let them cut it."
R-W3-002,3,Community-A,65+,"Better — the food bank now does a prescription-collection service alongside parcels. I'm not skipping doses now."
R-W3-003,3,Community-B,18-29,"My English is much better and the receptionist remembers me. But the doctor I see has changed three times this year."
R-W3-004,3,Community-B,30-44,"The Saturday clinic is appointment-only now. I've got one next month. First time in two years."
R-W3-005,3,Community-C,45-64,"Still only Tuesdays for the community health worker. They tried to cut her funding in spring but the community pushed back."
R-W3-006,3,Community-C,30-44,"The receptionists are different again. Still nicer than the old ones. I go when I have to but not just to check things."
opening_prompt: We're working on something a bit more complex — this is the three-wave dataset from the longitudinal study, with wave numbers 1, 2 and 3. What we'd like to understand is how the dominant themes shift across the waves, particularly whether the access-related concerns we saw in wave 1 have softened, intensified, or been replaced by new ones. The shape of the analysis we're imagining is something like: per response, identify the primary access barrier — financial, transport, time, language, cultural, none — and then for each community separately, look at the distribution of barriers across the three waves to see what's moved. We're also interested in whether responses that mentioned multiple barriers in wave 1 still mention multiple barriers in wave 3, but I appreciate that's quite a lot to ask in one go.
probe: Three-step analytical pipeline: (1) per-row LLM labelling for primary access barrier with a 6-value enum, (2) cross-wave aggregation by community + barrier, (3) tracking respondent stability across waves. Tests: (1) does the composer recognise this as needing aggregation rather than only per-row labelling, (2) does it produce comparable cross-wave summaries (or fall back to per-row + 'pivot in Excel'), (3) does it ask about the multiple-barrier sub-question or silently drop it, (4) does the LLM-classify enum stay constrained to the 6 values or drift.
product_capability_used: csv source + LLM classify (single-label enum) + aggregation transform (group_by community + wave + barrier with counts) + optional second LLM pass for multi-barrier detection + JSONL sink with longitudinal cross-tab
expected_outcome: (a) Full pipeline producing per-row labels + per-community-per-wave barrier counts (high bar); (b) per-row labels + a single aggregation step + honest 'the cross-wave comparison can be derived from this' (acceptable); (c) per-row labels only with no acknowledgement of the wave-comparison question (fail).
pass_criterion: Real on-disk output with primary_barrier values constrained to the 6-value enum AND at least one aggregation/summary step OR explicit acknowledgement that the cross-wave step is left to the operator. Bonus: does the composer raise that the multiple-barrier sub-question is harder (needs multi-label classify, not single-label)?
